onecli rules create \

--rate-limit 3 \
--rate-window 1h","content":"```bash
# Rate-limit Gmail API calls to 3 per hour

--rate-window 1h
```"}],"mentions":[{"start":57,"end":69,"username":"NanoClaw_AI"},{"start":0,"end":14,"username":"Gavriel_Cohen"}],"urls":[{"text":"https://github.com/onecli/onecli"},{"text":"https://github.com/qwibitai/nanoclaw"},{"text":"https://www.onecli.sh/blog/nanoclaw-adopts-agent-vault-credential-layer"},{"text":"https://nanoclaw.dev/blog/nanoclaw-agent-vault"},{"text":"https://www.onecli.sh/blog/credential-vault-ai-agent-security"},{"text":"https://x.com/ns123abc/status/2025975943529931240"},{"text":"https://x.com/ns123abc/status/2025975943529931240"}]},"media_entities":["13_2038253633440223232"],"cover_media":"3_2038250790259945472","preview_text":"Agents Don't Need Your API Keys
A director of AI alignment at Meta gave an agent access to her email. She told it not to take action without approval. It mass-deleted her inbox. She had to physically","plain_text":"Agents Don't Need Your API Keys
A director of AI alignment at Meta gave an agent access to her email. She told it not to take action without approval. It mass-deleted her inbox. She had to physically run to her computer to stop it.
I keep thinking about this story because the failure was not the agent's reasoning. It was the architecture. The agent had a raw API key. The system prompt said "ask before acting." The agent ignored the system prompt. There was nothing between the agent's decision and the Gmail API to enforce anything.
This is how almost every agent-to-service integration works today. Hand the agent a key. Hope the prompt holds. It does not always hold.
This is why I built Agent Vault in OneCLI, and this week @NanoClaw_AI (25k+ GitHub stars, one of the most used open-source agent frameworks) adopted it as their default credential layer.
 
What Agent Vault does
It sits between the agent and external services. The agent says "I need to call Gmail." The vault proxies the request and injects the real credential. The agent never sees the API key.
But credential proxying alone is table stakes. The part that matters is the policy layer on top.
Today that means rate limiting:
 
Go back to the Meta story. If that vault rule had been in place, the damage would have been three deleted emails, not hundreds. The agent still would have made the wrong decision. But the blast radius would have been completely different.
Why NanoClaw matters here
NanoClaw runs groups of cooperating agents. Multiple agents, shared goals, different capabilities. In that setup, credential management gets complicated fast. Which agent in the group can access what? What happens when one agent in the group goes off-script?
With Agent Vault, each NanoClaw agent group gets its own vault identity. Own credentials, own rules, own rate limits. An agent group doing research gets different policies than one managing your calendar.
@Gavriel_Cohen and the NanoClaw team wrote about their evaluation process and why they chose this path. It is worth reading: NanoClaw Adopts OneCLI Agent Vault.
What I am not claiming
A credential vault does not solve agent safety. It does not make agents smarter or more aligned. It reduces the blast radius of bad decisions that involve external services. That is a specific, limited, useful thing.
I wrote a whole post about this distinction ("What a Credential Vault Can and Can't Do for AI Agent Security ") and I stand by it. If someone tells you their tool solves agent security, they are selling you something. The best we can do right now is limit damage and maintain visibility.
What is coming
Rate limiting is live. Next up: time-bound access (credentials that expire) and approval workflows (vault pauses the request, asks a human for high-risk actions). Both are in active development.
Links
- OneCLI (open source): https://github.com/onecli/onecli
- NanoClaw: https://github.com/qwibitai/nanoclaw
- OneCLI's blog post: https://www.onecli.sh/blog/nanoclaw-adopts-agent-vault-credential-layer"}
